specific_location: Picket Wire Canyon, Comanche National Grassland; T27S, R55W, Sec. 26, SW 1/4; floodplain of Purgatoire River
description: Overall length was slightly shorter than nearby White-crowned Sparrows. The body was rounded, typical of small buntings - more junco-like than sparrow-like. The relative length of the tail was shorter than the sparrows. The head appeared slightly heavier than the sparrows relative to the body. The bill was sparrow-like - short, conical - and was a medium dark gray. The culmen was slightly convex, and the lower mandible was straight. For most of the observation the head was rounded, but there was a slight peak at the top of the head when the bird was alert. Head color was medium to dark blue, changing intensity with the angle of the light. The upper back was somewhat paler blue than the head, but the lower back had brownish-gray patches, and the rump was brownish-gray. The upper breast was about the same shade of blue as the upper back, but there was conspicuous mottling with the medium grayish-brown. Within the brownish-gray areas on the breast were noticeable darker gray, somewhat diffuse, streaks. The lower breast faded into pale gray in the undertail coverts. The tail was darker blue, more similar to the darker blue on the head. Flight feathers were about the same shade of blue. The wing coverts were brownish-gray, slightly darker that that of the breast on back. There were no obvious wingbars. The wings were short and rounded, as seen during short flights among the brush.Behaviors: The bunting was foraging among low brush and weeds. There were short flights from the ground into brush and among the brush. It was alert on the top of a low branch for a few seconds before disappearing. At that point the slight crest appeared at the center of the head, and the neck was extended, giving the bird a slightly longer appearance. It was among a flock of mixed sparrows including White-crowned, American Tree, Song, and Dark-eyed Junco. Also in the immediate vicinity were Pine Siskins, American Goldfinch, and Mountain Bluebird.Call: a short, sharp "spik!", heard twice during observationPlumage: winter adult
